attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a two-week history of progressive fatigue, shortness of breath on exertion, and jaundice. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears pale and jaundiced. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Cardiorespiratory examination is unremarkable. Abdominal examination reveals mild splenomegaly.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin: 68 g/L (115-165)

  • Mean cell volume: 102 fL (80-100)

  • White cell count: 11.2 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)

  • Platelet count: 280 x 10^9/L (150-400)

  • Reticulocyte count: 8% (0.5-2.5)

  • Bilirubin (unconjugated): 52 µmol/L (2-20)

  • Lactate dehydrogenase: 450 U/L (100-250)

  • Haptoglobin: <0.1 g/L (0.3-2.0)

Peripheral blood film shows numerous spherocytes.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical and laboratory features consistent with haemolytic anaemia. The most likely diagnosis based on the presentation is warm autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (AIHA). The direct antiglobulin test (DAT), also known as the direct Coombs test, is the most appropriate investigation to confirm this diagnosis. In warm AIHA, the DAT typically shows specificity for IgG antibodies. In contrast, in cold agglutinin disease, the DAT is typically positive for complement c3d.

A differential diagnosis in this case is hereditary spherocytosis, where haemolytic anaemia is present due to inherited defects in the red cell membrane. Spherocytes are also prominent on the blood film, commonly with associated hyposplenic features. However, the DAT is negative as it is not immune-mediated.

A bone marrow biopsy is not used to diagnose warm AIHA. It may be required in some cases to exclude causes of warm AIHA such as lymphoproliferative disorders, however it is not used to confirm the diagnosis of warm AIHA.

Serum protein electrophoresis is used to detect and identify monoclonal proteins, which can be associated with certain lymphoproliferative disorders or multiple myeloma. While some of these conditions can cause secondary AIHA, this test is not the most direct way to diagnose AIHA itself.

A full coagulation profile including tests such as prothrombin time (PT), activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT), and fibrinogen levels is not specifically indicated for diagnosing AIHA. While reduced fibrinogen and elevated D-dimer can occur in disseminated intravascular coagulation, a cause of microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, they are not a feature of AIHA and would not confirm the diagnosis.

Haemoglobin electrophoresis is used to diagnose haemoglobinopathies such as sickle cell disease or thalassemia. These conditions typically do not present acutely in adulthood and are not associated with the laboratory findings seen in this case.

The diagnosis of warm AIHA is supported by the presence of anaemia, reticulocytosis (accounting for the macrocytosis seen in this case), elevated unconjugated bilirubin, increased lactate dehydrogenase, decreased haptoglobin, and the presence of spherocytes on the peripheral blood film. These findings indicate ongoing haemolysis. The positive DAT confirms the autoimmune nature of the haemolysis, distinguishing it from other causes of haemolytic anaemia.
learningPoints: Warm autoimmune haemolytic anaemia is characterised by a positive direct antiglobulin test (DAT) for IgG antibodies.
Spherocytes on peripheral blood film are a characteristic finding in warm autoimmune haemolytic anaemia.
Elevated reticulocyte count, increased unconjugated bilirubin, and decreased haptoglobin are indicative of haemolysis.
references: Barcellini W, Fattizzo B. How I treat warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia [published correction appears in Blood. 2023 Jan 26;141(4):438-439. doi: 10.1182/blood.2022019024]. Blood. 2021;137(10):1283-1294. doi:10.1182/blood.2019003808
